title: The preschool bathroom: making ‘problem bodies’ and the limit of the disciplinary regime over children
abstract: AbstractIn this paper we study the effects of power in a bathroom, which is a rarely analysed space in preschools, using empirical examples from a semi-ethnographic study conducted in New South Wales, Australia. We demonstrate that educators' understanding and practices mostly consider their own positioning in discourses and come short in accounting for children's practices in and expressed views on the bathroom. Educators also remain distant from children's bodily experiences. The interplay of the open architectural design of the bathroom space and dominant discourses operating in the preschool constitute some children as 'problem bodies' apparently requiring (and justifying) direct intervention. Following this reasoning we argue that the surveillance, regularisation and normalisation in the bathroom is far from total, which leads us to question the adequacy of understanding the bathroom as forming a part of a modern (disciplinary) institution.Keywords: preschool bathroomearly childhooddisciplinary regimesFoucaultbiopedagogiesmodern institutions Notes1. The Commonwealth of Australia has six states and two major mainland territories. Early childhood provisions are administered by the State Governments and regulated and funded at Commonwealth level. There are many types of service provisions, each with their own nomenclature such as long daycare, kindergarten, childcare, preschool, and so forth. In order to avoid the complexity of terminology used and the confusion that it might bring, we call all early childhood provisions 'preschool' to cater for an international readership.2. See Australian Children's Education and Care Quality Authority's Education and Care Services National Law, National Quality Framework and Education and Care Services National Regulations at http://acecqa.gov.au/national-quality-framework/.3. Belonging, Being & Becoming: The Early Years Learning Framework (Australian Government Department of Education, Employment and Workplace Relations 2009), the first national curriculum framework in Australia, uniformly addresses teachers and carers with different qualifications to educators.4. This analysis draws on O'Laughlin's (Citation2006, 43) discussion of Sontag's (1997) argument in her book On Photography.
journal: British Journal of Sociology of Education
